coredata.dc:title: Facile and novel preparation of 2D-MoS2/carbon nanodots composite with high adsorption efficiency and capacity for dye removal
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Xianglu
Teng, Aijun
Zeng, Zehua
Meng, Hong
Wu, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Here, hydrophobic bulk molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) was efficiently exfoliated into two dimensional (2D) nanosheets assisted with amphiphilic carbon nanodots (CNDs) in pure water. The optimized concentration of obtained 2D-MoS2 is as high as 0.41 mg/mL, which is higher than that of reported literatures. The average lateral size and layer number of 2D-MoS2 is 142.6 (±2.3) nm and 4, respectively. The characterizations and simulation demonstrated that CNDs can be instantly adsorbed on the surface of 2D-MoS2 and form 2D-MoS2/CNDs composite. Thus, after standing for 1000 h, the concentration of 2D-MoS2 still accounts for 90 % of the initial concentration with the help of CNDs. Further, this technique can also be used to prepare graphene and 2D-WS2 nanosheets. 2D-MoS2/CNDs composite as adsorbent showed excellent adsorption capacity (1136.9 mg/g) and ultrafast adsorption rate (1 min) for methylene blue removal, which benefits from abundant adsorption sites and high dispersion. This work not only develops a facile and universal technique for green preparation of 2D nanomaterials in one pot, but also provides a promising adsorbent for removing organic pollutants from aqueous solution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Two dimension
Molybdenum disulfide
Carbon nanodots
Adsorbent

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Binding energy calculation of CNDs on MoS2 surface
Effects of adsorption time and initial concentration

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis spectra of as-prepared 2D-MoS2/CNDs dispersion with different exfoliation time and diluted with 25 times. Dispersed 2D-MoS2 concentration as a function of exfoliating time. (b) The 2D-MoS2 concentration as a function of exfoliation time when the pH is 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11, respectively. (c) The 2D-MoS2 concentration as a function of exfoliation time when the initial concentration of bulk MoS2 is 10, 30, 50, and 70 mg/mL, respectively. (d) Raman spectra of 2D-MoS2/CNDs and bulk MoS2.
(a) SEM image (b) length distribution of 2D-MoS2 and (c) TEM image of typical 2D-MoS2/CNDs. The inset in (c) is the partial enlarged detail.
(a) AFM image and height profile along with blue line of 2D-MoS2/CNDs (b) The layer numbers distribution of 2D-MoS2 nanosheets.
(a) Variation curve of 2D-MoS2 and 2D-MoS2/CNDs concentration with standing time. (b) Zeta potentials of CNDs, 2D-MoS2, and 2D-MoS2/CNDs and measured for three times.
(a) The binding energies of aromatic rings, hydroxyl, carboxyl, and epoxy on MoS2. (b) Schematic representation of CNDs as dispersing agent for exfoliating and dispersing MoS2 in pure water.
The UV–vis spectra of (a) 20 mg/L, (b) 40 mg/L of MB aqueous solution in presence of 2D-MoS2/CNDs composite.
(a) Removal rate and (b) adsorption capacity of 2D-MoS2/CNDs as function of adsorption time in different initial concentrations of MB solution.
Comparison of kinetic models for the adsorption of MB molecules onto the 2D-MoS2/CNDs by (a) Pseudo-first-order, (b) Pseudo-second-order, and (c) Intra-particle diffusion adsorption models.
(a) The adsorption capacity at the equilibrium plotted versus concentration of MB solution. (b) Langmuir, (c) Freundlich, and (d) D-R adsorption isotherm models of 2D-MoS2/CNDs.

head.ce:title: Facile and novel preparation of 2D-MoS2/carbon nanodots composite with high adsorption efficiency and capacity for dye removal

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ansteel Beijing Research Institute Co., LTD, Beijing 102211, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ansteel Beijing Research Institute Co., LTD

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ansteel Beijing Research Institute Co., LTD, Beijing 102211, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, hydrophobic bulk molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) was efficiently exfoliated into two dimensional (2D) nanosheets assisted with amphiphilic carbon nanodots (CNDs) in pure water. The optimized concentration of obtained 2D-MoS2 is as high as 0.41 mg/mL, which is higher than that of reported literatures. The average lateral size and layer number of 2D-MoS2 is 142.6 (±2.3) nm and 4, respectively. The characterizations and simulation demonstrated that CNDs can be instantly adsorbed on the surface of 2D-MoS2 and form 2D-MoS2/CNDs composite. Thus, after standing for 1000 h, the concentration of 2D-MoS2 still accounts for 90 % of the initial concentration with the help of CNDs. Further, this technique can also be used to prepare graphene and 2D-WS2 nanosheets. 2D-MoS2/CNDs composite as adsorbent showed excellent adsorption capacity (1136.9 mg/g) and ultrafast adsorption rate (1 min) for methylene blue removal, which benefits from abundant adsorption sites and high dispersion. This work not only develops a facile and universal technique for green preparation of 2D nanomaterials in one pot, but also provides a promising adsorbent for removing organic pollutants from aqueous solution.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Wastewater from textile, printing and other industries always contains a large number of toxic and non-volatile organics. If not removed in time, these organics will cause serious environmental pollution. Up to now, many technologies including adsorption [1–4], oxidation technique [5], coagulation [6,7] and biotechnique [8,9] have been developed for the removal of organics from wastewater. Among these methods, adsorption is considered to be the most convenient, economical, and efficient method for removing pollutants from wastewater [10,11]. Although various adsorbents with favourable adsorption capacity have been developed, such as zeolites, carbon materials, and metallic oxide, it is of great interest to develop a green absorbent with both higher adsorption capacity and faster adsorption rate.

Due to its the high surface area and abundant active sites, two-dimensional molybdenum disulfide (2D-MoS2) shows great potential for pollutant adsorption [12–16]. Khatri et al. [17] synthesized hierarchical microspheres of MoS2 nanosheets by hydrothermal reaction at 230 °C for 24 h. The adsorption capacity of synthesized MoS2 nanosheets is 297 mg/g for methylene blue (MB) at 400 min. Li et al. [18] synthesized layered porous MoS2 which have dye removal of 499.0 mg/g for MB at 420 min. Due to the strong hydrophobic nature, MoS2 usually takes a long time to reach adsorption equilibrium. In order to shorten the adsorption time, Liu et al. [19] prepared fungus-like MoS2/carbon nanodots composite through hydrothermal reaction at 220 °C for 12 h. The ultrasound was used to improve the dispersibility of composite. Thus, fungus-like MoS2/carbon nanodots composite quickly reached adsorption equilibrium at 2 s. However, the adsorption capacity is only 285 mg/g. The preparation of MoS2 nanosheets as adsorbent mainly adopts hydrothermal reaction at high temperature and long time, which has the characteristics of high energy consumption, low efficiency and difficulty in large-scale preparation. In addition, low dispersibility severely impairs the adsorption rate and adsorption capacity of MoS2 nanosheets. Thus, it is very necessary to facilely and effectively prepare MoS2 nanosheets with high dispersibility and high adsorption performance. Aqueous phase exfoliation (APE) is often used to prepare 2D-MoS2 nanosheets due to its facile and scalable process. A strong shear force is provided by ultrasonic or high-speed dispersive homogenizer is applied for exfoliating bulk MoS2 into 2D-MoS2 [20,21]. In this process, hydrophobic MoS2 must be dispersed in high boiling organic solvents or surfactant solutions. However, these organic solvents and surfactants tend to produce serious environmental pollution and inhibit the following applications. Carbon nanodots (CNDs) attracts ever-increasing attention due to their excellent properties such as amphipathy, chemical stability, high photoluminescent quantum yield, cost effectiveness, low cytotoxicity, and biocompatibility [22–26]. CNDs have been eco-friendly prepared from various carbon sources including glucose [27], glycine [28], and starch [22]. These carbon sources go through decomposition, dehydration and carbon atoms covalently interact with each other and form the carbon core. The carbon core with an aromatic structure exhibits hydrophobicity. It has been demonstrated that there is a strong attraction between aromatic structure and other hydrophobic material such as MoS2, WS2 and graphite by hydrophobic force and π-π interaction [29–31]. Meanwhile, the surface of CNDs contains many hydrophilic functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and epoxy groups, imparting CNDs with excellent water solubility [32]. Thus, CNDs have a great potential in exfoliating and dispersing hydrophobic MoS2 and forming 2D-MoS2/CNDs composite in one pot. In addition, it has been demonstrated that the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups of CNDs have a very strong adsorption effect on organics.

Herein, we applied CNDs as dispersing agent to achieve simple and efficient exfoliation of bulk MoS2 by APE, and obtained 2D-MoS2/CNDs composites in one pot. The first-principles calculation was applied for analyzing the mechanism of exfoliation. In addition, this technique also was successfully applied for producing other 2D-materials, such as 2D-WS2 and graphene. The morphology and dispersibility of 2D-MoS2/CNDs were analyzed in detail. It has been demonstrated that 2D-MoS2/CNDs exhibits high adsorption capacity and ultrafast adsorption rate for the removal of MB. The adsorption mechanism was systematacially analyzed by adsorption isotherm models and adsorption kinetic models.

Glucose and methylene blue were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). MoS2 (20 μm), WS2 (20 μm) and graphite (20 μm) were purchased from Shanghai Shen Yu (Shanghai, China). Deionized (DI) water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ·cm was lab-made and used for all experiments.

The CNDs were prepared by facile hydrothermal method. Glucose was used as carbon source due to low cost and environmentally friendly. First, 3.96 g of glucose was dissolved in 40 mL of DI water. The obtain solution was sealed in a 100 mL Teflon-sealed autoclave maintained at 160 °C. After 6 h, the solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 30 min. The collected supernatant is CNDs solution.

Typically, 14 g of bulk MoS2 were added to 200 mL CNDs solution. Then the suspension was shear exfoliated at 8000 rpm for 7 h by high-speed dispersive homogenizer. The obtained suspension was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min. The collected supernatant was dialyzed with a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 3 days. And then, 2D-MoS2/CNDs dispersion was obtained. The concentration of 2D-MoS2 in prepared dispersion was calculated from Lambert-Beer law. The adsorption coefficient is 2177.09 mL·mg−1·m−1. The effects of exfoliation time, initial concentration of bulk MoS2, and pH values on the exfoliation efficiency were investigated by controlled experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding energy calculation of CNDs on MoS2 surface
ce:section.ce:para: Density functional theory calculations were performed for analyzing the interaction between MoS2 and CNDs. The exchange and correlation potential is the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional and is treated using the generalized gradient approximation (GGA). The DFT + D2 scheme approach within the Grimme scheme is adopted for the vdW corrections. To uncover the interaction between MoS2 and CNDs, we calculated the binding energies of aromatic core and hydrophilic functional groups on MoS2 surface. The hydrophilic functional groups include hydroxyl, carboxyl, and epoxy groups. An 8 × 8 supercell of MoS2 monolayer composed of 64 molybdenum atoms and 128 sulphur atoms was constructed as substrate. A zigzag-edged C24H11 structure was used as aromatic structure and all edge carbon atoms were assumed to be fully passivated by H atoms to eliminate the dangling bonds. The different functional groups were added to the edge of aromatic structure, respectively as shown in Fig. S5. The energy cutoff of plane wave was set to 450 eV and the convergence tolerance for energy was 10-6 eV. All structures were relaxed until the force exerted on each atom was less than 0.01 eV/Å. The binding energy was calculated using the following equation: Eb = E(MoS2/T)-E(MoS2)-E(T), where E(MoS2/T), E(MoS2), and E(T) are the total energies of MoS2/T, MoS2, and T, respectively. The T is aromatic structure and aromatic structure with hydrophilic functional groups including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and epoxy groups, respectively. A more negative binding energy corresponds to a higher structural stability.

The adsorption capacity of 2D-MoS2/CNDs composite was evaluated by analyzing the reduction of cationic dye MB in aqueous solution. Typically, 2D-MoS2/CNDs dispersion was added to 100 mL MB solution (20 mg/L–140 mg/L) under stirring. The 5 mL mixing solution was took out at 1 min, 5 min, 10 min, 20 min, and 30 min, respectively. In quick succession, the mixing solutions were filtered by syringe-driven filter, and the 2D-MoS2/CNDs was removed. The concentration of MB was calculated by UV–vis spectra. The adsorption capacity qt (mg/g) and removal rate (%) were calculated according to the following equation [33].
(1)
q t
=
(
C 0
-
C t
) V
m
(2)
Removal rate
=
C 0
-
C t
C 0
× 100 %
where C0 and Ct were the MB initial concentration (mg/L) and instant concentration after adsorbing time t, respectively. The V and m were the volume (L) of MB solution and mass (mg) of adsorbent.

The UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a UV-5200PC spectrophotometer in quartz cuvettes with a path-length of 1 cm. The Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 360 spectrometer, and obtained from KBr pellet samples. The zeta potential was performed on Zetasizer Nano ZS 90. Raman spectra (Renishaw instrument) excited at 514 nm wavelength laser. The element contents of CNDs were obtained by Organic element analyzer (FlashSmart). The surface area was analyzed by ASAP2460. The morphologies of sample were observed with scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL6701F), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi HT7700 with 100 kV accelerating voltage), atomic force microscopy (AFM, DMFASTSCAN2-SYS instrument).

Firstly, the TEM, AFM, element analyzer, and FTIR spectra were performed to explain the morphology and chemical structure of prepared CNDs. Fig. S1a and S1b shows a typical TEM image of CNDs and the corresponding size distribution. CNDs are evenly distributed in the TEM image. There is no obvious agglomeration phenomenon, indicating that CNDs has very good dispersibility. The average size of CNDs is 2.03 ± 0.01 nm according to the Log-Normal fitting. Fig. S1c and S1d shows the thickness of CNDs is about 0.5 nm. Fig. S2 shows the FTIR spectra of CNDs for analyzing the chemical structure. The vibration band at 3401 cm−1 is assigned to OH stretching. Absorption bands at 2920 cm−1, 1704 cm−1 and 1385 cm−1 are assigned to CH, CO, and C–H2 stretching, respectively. And the absorption bands in 1000–1300 cm−1 are assigned to C–OH stretching and OH bending, indicating the existence of hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and epoxy groups. Further, absorption bands at 1622 cm−1 are assigned to CC. The absorption bands suggest that CNDs have a hydrophobic aromatic cores produced from aromatization of glucose during the hydrothermal treatment [27]. Fig. S3 indicates that the UV–vis spectrum of CNDs has two shoulder peaks which are consistent with other reports [34,35]. The absorption at 268 nm can be attributed to the π-π* transition of the conjugated CC structure. And the absorption at 345 nm is assigned to the n-π* transition of the surface functional groups. Table S1 shows the ratio of oxygen and carbon is 0.387. These results proved that the prepared CNDs are amphiphilic with both surface hydrophilic functional groups and hydrophobic aromatic cores.

It has been demonstrated that the prepared CNDs is amphiphilic in above section. The core of CNDs contains hydrophobic aromatic rings. The surface of CNDs has hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups. Thus, we utilized CNDs as dispersing agent for exfoliation of bulk MoS2 in the process of APE. Fig. 1 a shows the UV–vis spectra of prepared dispersion at different exfoliation time. Two extinct absorption peaks at 606.4 nm and 663.2 nm which are assigned to the characteristic B and A direct excitonic transitions of 2D-MoS2 with the energy split from the valence band spin-orbital coupling as well as the other features consistent with the 2H polytype of 2D-MoS2 [36]. This result confirms that bulk MoS2 was successfully exfoliated into 2D structure in CNDs solution. The concentration of 2D-MoS2 can also be calculated accord to the Lambert beer’s law. The extinction coefficient of 2D-MoS2 at 663.2 nm is 2177.09 mL·mg−1·m−1 [37]. Fig. 1b shows the concentration of 2D-MoS2 as a function of exfoliation time at different pH values. As we known, pH value has a strong effect on the stability of hydrophilic groups, especially for carboxyl groups. Thus, the effect of pH value on exfoliation efficiency was firstly investigated. When the pH value is 5 or 7, the concentration of obtained 2D-MoS2 achieve the vertices because of the high stability of CNDs. The initial concentration of bulk MoS2 and exfoliation time were also optimized as shown in Fig. 1c. The results show a similar tendency which common surfactants such as sodium cholate and Triton-100 were used for exfoliating layered materials [38]. Thus the optimum parameters are 5 of pH value, 70 mg/mL of bulk MoS2, and 7 h of exfoliation time. The optimum concentration of 2D-MoS2 is 0.41 mg/mL, which are comparable and even better to some of reports about using common surfactant for preparing 2D-MoS2 as shown in Table S2. In order to further confirm the nature of dispersions, Raman spectra of dispersion are shown in Fig. 1d. Two distinct peaks centred at 381.2 cm−1 and 406.4 cm−1, which correspond to the in-plane low energy E2g mode and out-of-plane high-energy A1g mode of MoS2, respectively. No obvious peaks shifts compared to bulk MoS2 are observed. This is because randomly restacked nanosheets and few-layered structure [39]. The strong peak intensity and no unexpected vibrations suggest that 2D-MoS2 has a high crystallinity. Beyond that, the spectrum of prepared dispersion shows the d-band centred at ∼1350 cm−1 and G-band centred at ∼1583 cm−1, which derived from added CNDs. The d-band arises from the violation of the momentum conservation law due to the formation of defects or edge states (zigzag and armchair). Meanwhile, G-band as graphitic is attributed to the in-plane lattice vibrations of sp2 carbon. Fig. S4 shows the FT-IR spectra of MoS2 and 2D-MoS2/CNDs. The FT-IR spectra both have a band at about 470 cm−1 which corresponds to Mo-S vibration. In addition, compared to MoS2, 2D-MoS2/CNDs exhibits very similar absorptions of CNDs. These results indicate that 2D-MoS2/CNDs composite were prepared in water by APE.

Fig. 2 a and b shows the typical SEM image and corresponding length distribution of 2D-MoS2. 2D-MoS2 nanosheets have an ultrathin structure compared with bulk MoS2 (Fig. S5). The corresponding length distribution of 2D-MoS2 are 142.6 ± 2.3 nm. The surface area of 2D-MoS2/CNDs is 15.4 m2/g, which is greater than 2.3 m2/g of bulk MoS2. As show in figrue 2c, the basal plane and edge of 2D-MoS2 nanosheets were covered by a mass of CNDs, suggesting that they are compatible and can be combined to spontaneously form 2D-MoS2/CNDs heterostructure. Fig. S6 shows the high-resolution TEM image and Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) of 2D-MoS2. The lattice spacing is 0.27 nm, which is consistent with the (100) lattice plane of MoS2. The FFT indicates that 2D-MoS2 have a hexagonally symmetric structure with excellent crystallinity.

Fig. 3 a shows the typical AFM image of 2D-MoS2/CNDs. The morphology of these nanosheets are consistent with the analyzation of the TEM images. We measured the layer number of ∼100 nanosheets observed in the AFM and the data presented in Fig. 3b as a histogram. The histogram shows the prepared nanosheets to be thinner than 7 layers with an average layer number of 4 [40]. This is reasonably layer numbers and comparable to 2D-MoS2 exfoliated in common surfactant aqueous solution (sodium cholate, Triton-100, and protein) and organic solvents (N-methyl-pyrrolidone) which have thickness between 1 and 10 layers [16,41–43]. In addition, the obtained 2D-MoS2 nanosheets are uniformly attached to the Si substrate after deposition, indicating that 2D-MoS2/CNDs hybrids have excellent dispersibility and stability in pure water.

Pure 2D-MoS2 nanosheets have a poor stability and dispersibility in pure water due to the high surface energy. Fig. 4 a shows the change of concentration as a function of standing time, where the C0 is the initial concentration of dispersion and the C is the instant concentration after standing. It can be seen that 2D-MoS2 nanosheets have obvious agglomeration phenomenon in pure water. After standing for 1000 h, the concentration only accounts for 40 % of the initial concentration. The concentration is only 20 % left, after 3500 h. However, 2D-MoS2/CNDs dispersion have a slight sedimentation within 1000 h. And after 1500 h, the value of C/C0 still remains above 71 %. These results indicate that 2D-MoS2/CNDs have a excellent dispersion stability in pure water. This will be beneficial to practical application and storage. Zeta potential was measured for further analyzing the stability of 2D-MoS2/CNDs. Fig. 4b shows that the zeta potentials of 2D-MoS2, CNDs, and 2D-MoS2/CNDs dispersions. These dispersions have a same concentration for investigating the change of zeta potential. It can be seen that the zeta potential of 2D-MoS2 is greatly enhanced by CNDs and up to −50.1 mV. The strong negative zeta potential resulted from ionization of CNDs makes 2D-MoS2 has enough electrostatic repulsive force to prevent the agglomeration of 2D-MoS2 nanosheets. CNDs play a key role in dispersing and stabilizing 2D-MoS2 in pure water.

To theoretically understand the interaction between 2D-MoS2 and CNDs, density functional theory (DFT) was used to simulate the binding energies of aromatic rings with hydrophilic functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, and epoxy) on MoS2. A more negative binding energy corresponds to a higher structural stability and a spontaneous bonding. Fig. 5 a shows that the binding energy of aromatic rings on MoS2 layer is −1.21 eV, which is much lower than that of two adjacent MoS2 layers (−0.28 eV). Notably, when the hydrophilic functional groups were added to the aromatic rings, it still has strong binding energy on MoS2 layer. Thus, the preparation mechanism of 2D-MoS2/CNDs can be explain as shown in Fig. 5b. After adding CNDs and MoS2 in pure water, CNDs would spontaneously bind on the surface of MoS2, facilitating the aqueous phase exfoliation of bulk MoS2 and forming 2D-MoS2/CNDs composite materials. The hydrophobic MoS2 is steadily dispersed in water with the assist of CNDs.

In addition, we further demonstrated that CNDs as dispersing agent can be also used to exfoliate and disperse other layered materials. Fig. S8a shows the TEM image of prepared graphene. Graphene shows transparent and folded morphology due to its ultrathin structure. The length of graphene is about several micrometres. Fig. S8b shows the raman spectrum of obtained graphene with excitation at 514 nm. The Raman spectra exhibits three typical peaks at about 1354.9 cm−1, 1580.3 cm−1, and 2717.6 cm−1, corresponding to the D, G, and 2D bands of graphene, respectively. The D/G intensity ratio is proportional to degree of graphene defect. The ID/IG of obtained graphene is 0.23 consistent with that of reported graphene by APE. The low value of ID/IG indicates that graphene has few defects and perfect structure. Fig. S8c show the prepared 2D-WS2 nanosheets with a transparent ultrathin structure. The length of 2D-WS2 nanosheets is 100 nm to 400 nm. In Fig. S8d, the UV–vis spectrum of 2D-WS2 dispersion shows a distinct absorption peak at 633 nm, which is attributed to the materials size decreased from the bulk WS2 with indirect band gap to 2D-WS2 with direct band gap.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of adsorption time and initial concentration
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption performance of 2D-MoS2/CNDs composites was analyzed by adsorption MB solution at room temperature. Fig. 6 shows the UV–vis spectra of MB solution (20 mg/L and 40 mg/L, respectively) as function of adsorption time. It can be seen that 2D-MoS2/CNDs composite quickly adsorb MB molecules from solution and reach adsorption dynamic equilibrium within 1 min. Comparing to the reported adsorbent, the adsorption rate of 2D-MoS2/CNDs is highest as shown in Table S3. Fig. 7 shows the removal rate and adsorption capacity of 2D-MoS2/CNDs in different initial concentrations of MB solution. In 20 mg/L and 40 mg/L of MB solution, 2D-MoS2/CNDs exhibits the highest removal rates at 92 %. When the concentration is greater than 60 mg/L, the removal rate gradually decreases with the increase of the initial MB concentration. The removal rate can still reach 80 % at 140 mg/L of MB solution. The adsorption capacity of the 2D-MoS2/CNDs increased gradually with the increase of the initial concentration of MB solution as shown in Fig. 7b. When the MB initial concentration was 140 mg/L, the adsorption capacity reached the largest value of 1136.9 mg/g. The abundant oxygen-containing functional groups (–OH, –COOH, and –CO) of CNDs and 2D-MoS2 nanosheets endow 2D-MoS2/CNDs with ultra-high adsorption capacity. In addition, the ultrafast adsorption rate of 2D-MoS2/CNDs is attributed to the excellent dispersibility in water which benefits to MB molecules quickly adsorption on surface active sites of 2D-MoS2/CNDs.

The pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models were applied for analyzing the adsorption kinetic of MB molecules onto 2D-MoS2/CNDs composite. The intra-particle mass transfer diffusion model was used for explaining the diffusion mechanism of adsorption process. The pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order kinetic model and intra-particle mass transfer diffusion model is expressed by the following equation [44], respectively:
l n
q e
-
q t
= l n
q e
-
K 1
2.303
t
t
q t
1
K 2
q e 2
= +
t
q e

q t = Kit0.5 + C.where q e and q t are the amounts of adsorbed MB molecules (mg·g−1) at adsorption equilibrium and adsorption time t (min), respectively. K1 is the rate constant of the pseudo first-order kinetic model (min−1). K2 is the rate constant of the pseudo-second-order kinetic model (g·mg−1·min−1). Ki is the intra-particle diffusion rate constant (mg·g−1·min−0.5). C is the ordinate intercept.

Fig. 8 shows the fitted curves of pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order kinetic model and intra-particle mass transfer diffusion model at different concentrations. The calculated relevant parameters are listed in Table S4. The determination coefficient (R2) of pseudo-second-order is much higher than that of pseudo-first-order model. The calculated q e accord to pseudo-second-order model are close to the experimental ones. These results indicates that the pseudo-second-order model is more appropriate to explain the adsorption behavior of MB molecules onto 2D-MoS2/CNDs. It can be refers that the rate-limiting step in the adsorption process is chemical adsorption. The exchange or sharing of electrons occurs between the cationic MB and the anion active site (–OH, –COOH, and –CO on the surface of CNDs and 2D-MoS2 nanosheets).

The adsorption isotherms are of great significance for analyzing adsorption behavior and evaluating adsorption performance. In this work, the experimental data was treated by Langmuir, Freundlich, and Dubinin-Radushkevich isotherm models. They are described by following equation [44], respectively:
C e/Q e = C e/Q m + 1/(K L· Q m)
ln Q e = ln K F + (1/n)ln C e
ln Q e = ln Q m − βƐ 2
where C e and Q e are the amount of unadsorbed MB and adsorbed MB concentration (mg/g) at equilibrium, respectively. KL (L/mg) is the Langmuir constant. Q m (mg/g) is the maximum adsorption capacity of the adsorbent corresponding to the complete monolayer coverage. KF and 1/n are Freundlich constants related to adsorption capacity and adsorption intensity. β (mol2/kJ2) is the D-R isotherm constant related to the free energy of adsorption. Ɛ is the Polanyi potential.

Fig. 9 shows the isotherm data obtained were fitted by above three isotherm models. The adsorption isotherm parameters and the correlation coefficient (R2) were listed in Table S5. According to R2 values of three adsorption isotherm equations, the Langmuir isotherm fitted the experimental data better than all other models. This results demonstrated that the adsorptions of MB molecules on 2D-MoS2/CNDs are monolayer uniform adsorptions. In addition, the KL values calculated from Langmuir equation located at the range of 0–1, indicates that 2D-MoS2/CNDs is suitable for adsorption of MB molecules from aqueous medium. The value of 1/n calculated from Freundlich equation, which represent the favorable nature of adsorption.

In summary, we accomplished the facile and green preparation of 2D-MoS2/CNDs in one pot by aqueous phase exfoliation. The CNDs were used as dispersing agent instead of surfactants or organic solvents, which is conducive to the following applications. In addition, the optimized concentration of 2D-MoS2 is as high as 0.41 mg/mL, which is higher than that of reported literatures. The experimental results and DFT simulation demonstrated that CNDs can spontaneously adsorb on MoS2 layers for forming composite and then greatly improving dispersibility of 2D-MoS2 in pure water. This technique has also been successfully used to prepare graphene and 2D-WS2. The resulting 2D-MoS2/CNDs composite showed excellent adsorption performance to MB, owing to the high dispersibility and abundant adsorption sites. The adsorption of MB on the 2D-MoS2/CNDs conforms to the monolayer adsorption model and pseudo-second-order kinetic model. This work developed a facile and universal technique for aqueous phase exfoliation of hydrophobic materials in pure water. In addition, the prepared 2D-MoS2/CNDs with outstanding adsorption properties may be a promising adsorbent for fast and efficient purification of wastewater.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21676023).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.138319.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
